name: Me of Enki
description: The Me of Enki are a collection of divine decrees, fundamental rules, social institutions, religious practices, technologies, arts, crafts, and all aspects of civilization, culture, and existence, believed to be held by the god Enki, the god of wisdom, intelligence, crafts, mischief, water, and creation. These Me are not physical objects, but rather abstract concepts or blueprints for how things should be in the world. They represent the very essence of civilization and order. Enki, in some myths, generously bestows these Me upon humanity, allowing them to develop and flourish. However, in the myth of Inanna and Enki, Inanna cunningly steals the Me from Enki, transferring them from his city of Eridu to her city of Uruk, thus granting Uruk power and prominence. The loss of the Me would have devastating consequences for any city or civilization. Their control signifies power, influence, and the ability to shape the world. The Me encompass everything from kingship and priesthood to writing, music, and metalworking, effectively defining the structure and function of Sumerian society. The number of Me is often cited as being around one hundred, although the exact list varies depending on the source. They are the foundation of Sumerian culture and the basis of their advanced civilization, representing the divine order established by the gods.
subtitle: Divine decrees shaping Sumerian civilization
origin: Created by the gods, held by Enki
currentLocation: Abstract; potentially dispersed or held in Uruk after Inanna's theft.

keyMyths.title: Inanna and Enki
keyMyths.description: Inanna travels to Eridu and, through trickery or persuasion, obtains the Me from Enki. She then brings them back to Uruk, granting her city significant power and influence.

keyMyths.title: Enki's Wisdom
keyMyths.description: Enki, as the god of wisdom, inherently possesses and understands the Me. He uses this knowledge to guide humanity and establish the foundations of civilization.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
sources.description: A foundational text on Sumerian myths and legends.
sources.text: A foundational text on Sumerian myths and legends.

sources.author: Thorkild Jacobsen
sources.work: The Treasures of Darkness: A History of Mesopotamian Religion
sources.description: Provides insights into the religious and cultural context of the Me.
sources.text: Provides insights into the religious and cultural context of the Me.

worshipPractices.name: Invocation of Enki's Wisdom
worshipPractices.description: Sumerians would invoke Enki's wisdom and mastery of the Me through prayers and incantations. These rituals sought to gain insight into the proper application of the Me for the benefit of their city and its people, often involving offerings of water and crafted items.

worshipPractices.name: Celebration of Crafts and Innovation
worshipPractices.description: Acknowledging Enki's role as the patron of crafts and innovation, artisans and inventors would dedicate their creations and skills to him. This dedication involved rituals of purification and blessings, ensuring the Me were properly utilized in their endeavors.

festivals.name: The Festival of Enki's Wisdom
festivals.description: This festival celebrated Enki's gift of civilization and the proper use of the Me. It involved feasts, storytelling, and the showcasing of new inventions and advancements in agriculture and crafts, symbolizing the continued flourishing of Sumerian society under Enki's guidance.

festivals.name: The Descent and Ascent of Inanna
festivals.description: While focusing on Inanna's acquisition of the Me, the festival indirectly honored Enki's role as the holder and distributor of these divine decrees. It recounted the story of Inanna's journey to the underworld and her eventual return with the Me, highlighting the importance of balance and the proper application of knowledge.

cultural.modernLegacy: The concept of the Me of Enki continues to inspire discussions about the foundations of civilization and the responsible use of technology. They appear in modern fantasy literature and games as powerful artifacts representing knowledge and control over the fundamental forces of the universe, reflecting humanity's ongoing fascination with the origins of culture and innovation.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like the Me, Pandora's Box contained essential, if dangerous, aspects of existence. While the Me were meant to structure civilization, the Box contained evils and blessings that shaped human life after its opening.

Ma'at embodies the concepts of truth, justice, balance, and cosmic order, similar to how the Me of Enki represent the foundational principles upon which civilization is built. Both represent divinely ordained structures for a functioning society.

The Mead of Poetry granted wisdom and poetic inspiration to those who drank it, much like the Me of Enki represented the knowledge and skills necessary for a flourishing civilization. Both are sources of profound knowledge and creative power.

The Vedas are a collection of sacred texts that contain knowledge about rituals, philosophy, and the nature of reality, similar to how the Me contain the fundamental principles of civilization. Both serve as a foundation of knowledge and cultural identity.

associations.name: Civilization
associations.significance: The Me are the building blocks of civilization, establishing order and structure.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Enki is associated with water, symbolizing the source of life and the foundation of civilization.

associations.name: Seven
associations.significance: Seven is a sacred number in Sumerian mythology, often associated with completeness and divine power, reflecting the comprehensive nature of the Me.

associations.name: Knowledge
associations.significance: The Me embody the entirety of knowledge needed for a functioning society.

associations.name: Fish
associations.significance: Fish are associated with Enki's watery domain and symbolize the abundance and nourishment that the Me provide to civilization.

associations.name: Authority
associations.significance: The Me represent the divine authority and power held by Enki to govern the world.

associations.name: Reed
associations.significance: Reeds are used for writing and building, symbolizing the laws and structures the Me of Enki represent.

companions.name: Ninhursag
companions.description: Ninhursag is a mother goddess and Enki's consort in some myths. She often works with Enki to shape and nurture humanity.

companions.name: Isimud
companions.description: Isimud is Enki's two-faced vizier, a loyal servant and messenger. He carries Enki's instructions and acts as an intermediary.

companions.name: Adapa
companions.description: Adapa is a mortal sage and priest of Enki, blessed with great wisdom by his patron god. Enki protects Adapa from the curse of immortality.

companions.name: Asnan
companions.description: Asnan is the goddess of grain, created by Enki and Ninhursag to provide food for humanity. She represents one of the gifts of civilization contained within the Me.

companions.name: Ninki
companions.description: Ninki is Enki's wife. She is the goddess of the earth.

extendedContent.title: The Nature and Scope of the Me
extendedContent.content:
The Me of Enki represent a comprehensive catalog of cosmic and cultural laws, defining the very fabric of Sumerian society and the natural world. They are not merely abstract principles, but rather active forces that shape reality. Enki, as the god of wisdom and crafts, held dominion over these fundamental decrees, controlling their distribution and application. This control was seen as essential for maintaining order and prosperity. The Me encompass everything from kingship and priesthood to specific crafts like weaving and metalworking, ensuring that each aspect of life operated according to divine will.

Sumerian texts describe the Me as being housed in Enki's temple in Eridu, a city considered the oldest and most sacred in Sumerian civilization. This association reinforces Enki's role as the source of all knowledge and progress. The theft of the Me by Inanna, as described in the myth "Inanna and Enki," highlights their immense value and the power associated with their possession. The story illustrates the pivotal role of the Me in the advancement of civilization and the competition between deities for their control.

The concept of the Me transcends mere rules; they embody the inherent nature of things, their purpose, and their proper function. Understanding and adhering to the Me was crucial for maintaining balance in the cosmos and ensuring the well-being of society. The Me provided a framework for ethical behavior, social organization, and technological advancement, guiding the Sumerians in their quest to build a prosperous and harmonious civilization. They are fundamental to understanding Sumerian cosmology and their worldview.

extendedContent.title: Enki's Role as Guardian and Distributor
extendedContent.content:
Enki's position as the holder and distributor of the Me is central to his character and his importance in the Sumerian pantheon. His wisdom and intelligence made him uniquely qualified to understand and manage these powerful decrees. He was not simply a keeper of the Me, but also their interpreter and implementer, ensuring that they were used for the benefit of humankind. The myths portray him as a benevolent figure, even when his actions appear mischievous, as his ultimate goal is always to advance civilization.

The story of "Enki and the World Order" further emphasizes his role in establishing the structure of the universe and assigning duties to the other gods. In this narrative, Enki meticulously plans the organization of the cosmos, assigning specific responsibilities to each deity, including the control of rivers, fields, and even specific aspects of human life. This act of divine planning solidifies Enki's position as the architect of civilization and the guarantor of cosmic order.

However, Enki's guardianship of the Me was not without its challenges. The myth of Inanna's theft demonstrates the constant threat of chaos and the need for vigilance in protecting these fundamental decrees. Enki's actions, both in initially granting the Me to Inanna and later attempting to retrieve them, reveal the complexities of his role and the delicate balance between sharing knowledge and maintaining control. His actions highlight the inherent tension between empowering others and safeguarding the very foundations of Sumerian society.

extendedContent.title: Inanna's Acquisition: A Tale of Power and Progress
extendedContent.content:
The myth of "Inanna and Enki" provides a crucial insight into the dynamic between the gods and the transfer of power and knowledge. Inanna, the goddess of love, fertility, and warfare, desires the Me to elevate her city of Uruk to prominence. Through a cunning and arguably deceptive strategy, she manages to convince Enki to grant her the Me during a drunken feast. This act of acquisition is not portrayed as a simple theft, but rather as a calculated move to advance her own city and enhance her own power.

The narrative details the various Me that Inanna obtains, ranging from kingship and priesthood to specific crafts and artistic skills. This comprehensive list underscores the breadth and depth of the Me and their significance in shaping civilization. As Inanna sails back to Uruk with her prize, Enki realizes the magnitude of his loss and attempts to retrieve the Me, sending various creatures to intercept her boat. This chase highlights the intense competition between the gods for control over these fundamental decrees.

Ultimately, Inanna successfully brings the Me to Uruk, ushering in a new era of prosperity and cultural advancement for the city. The myth serves as a powerful allegory for the transfer of knowledge and the role of ambition in driving progress. While Enki is portrayed as the original holder of the Me, Inanna's acquisition demonstrates the potential for change and the importance of adapting to new circumstances. The story highlights the dynamic relationship between tradition and innovation, and the constant struggle for power and influence in the divine realm.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism and Cultural Significance
extendedContent.content:
The Me of Enki are deeply symbolic, representing the very essence of Sumerian culture and civilization. They embody the values, beliefs, and practices that shaped their society and defined their worldview. The concept of the Me reflects the Sumerian belief in a divinely ordained order and the importance of adhering to that order for maintaining harmony and prosperity. The Me are not simply abstract principles; they are active forces that shape reality and influence human behavior.

The association of the Me with Enki's temple in Eridu reinforces the city's status as a center of knowledge and wisdom. Eridu was considered the oldest and most sacred city in Sumerian civilization, and its connection to the Me further solidified its importance as a place of divine inspiration and cultural innovation. The myths surrounding the Me highlight the significance of knowledge and the power associated with its possession. The competition between the gods for control over the Me reflects the human desire for understanding and the struggle for dominance in the world.

The concept of the Me continues to resonate with modern scholars and researchers who seek to understand the origins of civilization and the development of human culture. The Me provide valuable insights into the values, beliefs, and practices of the Sumerians, offering a glimpse into the minds of one of the earliest and most influential civilizations in human history. The study of the Me allows us to appreciate the complexity and sophistication of Sumerian thought and their lasting contribution to the development of human society.

shortDescription: The Me of Enki are not physical objects, but rather represent the totality of divine decrees, cultural practices, technologies, and fundamental laws governing Sumerian civilization, all held by Enki.

wielders.name: Enki
wielders.context: Primary holder and distributor of the Me, bestowing civilization upon humanity.

wielders.name: Inanna
wielders.context: Goddess who cunningly acquired many Me from Enki, transferring them to her city of Uruk.

wielders.name: Humans
wielders.context: Beneficiaries of the Me, receiving the gifts of civilization through Enki and Inanna.
